The adoption of Enterprise AI has slowed down active deployment in 42% of large companies (more than 1,000 employees), while 40% remain stuck in exploration or experimentation phases and are unable to overcome persistent barriers that prevent them from realizing early emerging operational benefits.
This bifurcation plays a role because the early adopters are making further progress – 59% of companies that are already active or are researching it.
AI has accelerated its investment in the last 24 months – a competitive gap that will become more and more difficult for later people to close.

The biggest obstacle is a shortage of skilled workers: 33% of companies cite limited AI know-how as a top inhibitor, one in five reports that employees with the right skills are missing, and 16% do not find qualified new jobs to fill the gap – currently only 34% of their workforce are retrained.
Data complexity blocks 25% of organizations, ethical concerns block 23%, integration difficulties and high cost of each block about 21%.
Generative AI is facing even steeper barriers of trust, with 57% of non-adopters referring to data protection and 43% referring to transparency concerns.
Geographical diversity is strong: India (59%), the UAE (58%), Singapore (53%), France (26%), Spain (28%) and Australia (29%).
Financial services are leading in industry with around 50% use.
Those using AI include IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance or monitoring (25%).

Accessibility is the primary adoption accelerator – 45% of respondents say AI tools are easier to use than adoption, followed by cost reduction needs (42%) and AI embedded in off-the-shelf software (37%).

85% of IT professionals agree that consumers prefer companies with transparent, ethical AI practices, but less than half have a distortion reduction (27%), a data-provenence tracking (37%) or an ethical AI policy (44%).

Organisations should prioritise structured training programmes for workers, which are currently carried out by only one-third of enterprises, as the skills gap is the most frequently cited barrier, it combines data complexity and integration challenges and the 59% acceleration rate among current users shows that, once they have sufficient expertise, teams follow investment and deployment rapidly.
